enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source log analyzer that turns Apache, IIS, FTP, and mail server logs into graphical traffic reports.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: AWStats is a server log analysis tool that parses raw web, FTP, and mail server logs and generates advanced, graphical usage reports. Rather than tracking visitors with client-side JavaScript, it works entirely from log files already produced by Apache, IIS, WebStar, and other servers, so there is no tracking script to add to a site.

Created by Laurent Destailleur and released under the GNU General Public License, AWStats has been a staple of shared-hosting control panels for years. Version 8.0, released in August 2025, was noted by the original author as the last release he personally plans to maintain, with community contributors continuing development on GitHub. The author has publicly pointed users who want an actively developed alternative toward Matomo.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: AWStats suits hosting providers and system administrators who already have server log files and want a lightweight, no-JavaScript way to review traffic, errors, and search engine referrals without deploying a database-backed analytics stack.
It is less suited to teams that need real-time dashboards, event tracking, or modern product analytics — its reports are generated from static log parsing, typically run on a schedule.

features.title: Log-based analytics
features.description: Parses Apache, IIS, WebStar, FTP, and mail server logs directly instead of relying on JavaScript trackers.

features.title: Visitor and geography reports
features.description: Reports unique visitors, visit duration, and country of origin based on IP-to-country detection.

features.title: Browser and OS detection
features.description: Identifies visiting browsers and operating systems from user-agent strings.

features.title: Search engine and keyword tracking
features.description: Recognizes referrals and search keywords from a large list of known search engines.

features.title: Robot and error detection
features.description: Flags bot/crawler traffic and reports HTTP errors found in the logs.

features.title: CLI or CGI operation
features.description: Runs as a command-line batch job or as a CGI application with dynamic, interactive filtering.

features.title: Multi-format export
features.description: Exports reports to HTML, XHTML, and PDF.

features.title: Load-balanced cluster support
features.description: Can merge logs from multiple servers in a load-balanced setup into a single report.

pros: Completely free and open source under the GPL
No JavaScript tracker required — works from existing server logs
Long-standing tool widely supported by hosting control panels
Detailed browser, OS, and search-engine breakdowns out of the box
cons: No longer actively maintained by its original author as of the 8.0 release
Report generation is log-based/batch rather than real-time
Interface and reporting style feel dated compared to modern analytics tools
Requires server-level access to log files and a Perl environment

faqs.question: Is AWStats free?
faqs.answer: Yes. AWStats is free, open-source software released under the GNU General Public License.

faqs.question: Does AWStats require a JavaScript tracking snippet?
faqs.answer: No. AWStats generates reports by parsing existing server log files, so no client-side tracking code is needed.

faqs.question: Is AWStats still maintained?
faqs.answer: The original author released version 8.0 in August 2025 and stated it would be his last personal release; community maintenance continues on GitHub, but active development has slowed.

faqs.question: What server logs can AWStats read?
faqs.answer: It supports Apache, IIS, WebStar, FTP, and mail server log formats, among others.

use_cases.title: Shared hosting traffic reports
use_cases.description: Hosting providers bundle AWStats to give customers basic traffic stats without running a separate analytics service.

use_cases.title: Lightweight log auditing
use_cases.description: Sysadmins use AWStats to review error rates, bot traffic, and referrer sources directly from existing logs.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: AWStats: free, open-source log analyzer that turns Apache, IIS, FTP, and mail logs into graphical traffic and visitor reports.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source edition of Countly's product and web analytics platform for session, event, and crash tracking.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Countly Community Edition (also referred to as Countly Lite) is the free, self-hosted version of the Countly analytics platform. It collects sessions, views, custom events, and crash/error reports from web, mobile, and other SDKs, and presents them through built-in dashboards and reports that an organization runs entirely on its own infrastructure.

It is released under an AGPL-3.0 license with a modified Section 7, and can be installed via an automated script on Ubuntu/CentOS/RHEL, via Docker, or from stable release packages. Countly also sells hosted (Countly Flex) and on-premise Enterprise editions with additional features such as granular data access controls, SLAs, and dedicated support, which are separate commercial products from the Community Edition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Countly Community Edition is aimed at individuals and small teams who want to self-host product/web analytics and crash reporting rather than pay for the hosted or Enterprise Countly plans, and who are comfortable maintaining their own server infrastructure.

features.title: Sessions, views, and events
features.description: Collects session data, page/screen views, and custom events across web and mobile SDKs.

features.title: Crash and error reporting
features.description: Captures crash and error reports from SDKs for iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, Node.js, Unity, Java, and JavaScript.

features.title: Push notifications
features.description: Sends push notifications to iOS and Android apps from the self-hosted dashboard.

features.title: Remote configuration
features.description: Supports remote config to change app behavior without shipping a new release.

features.title: In-app ratings
features.description: Includes customizable in-app rating widgets.

features.title: Dashboards and email reports
features.description: Provides built-in, customizable reports and dashboards plus scheduled email reports and alerts.

features.title: Webhooks and APIs
features.description: Offers data webhooks and read/write APIs for integrating Countly data with other systems.

features.title: Plugin architecture
features.description: A plugin-based system allows extending core functionality.

pros: Free to self-host with full source access
Broad SDK coverage for web, mobile, and desktop platforms
Includes crash reporting and push notifications alongside analytics
Plugin architecture for extending functionality
cons: Requires operating and maintaining your own server infrastructure
AGPL license with modified terms may need legal review for some commercial uses
Lacks advanced access controls, SLAs, and support available in the paid Enterprise edition
Initial setup is more involved than a hosted analytics SaaS

faqs.question: Is Countly Community Edition free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open source under an AGPL-3.0 license with a modified Section 7.

faqs.question: How is Community Edition different from Countly Enterprise?
faqs.answer: Community Edition (Lite) is self-hosted only and lacks features like granular data access, dedicated SLA, and direct support that come with the paid Enterprise on-premise or Countly Flex hosted offerings.

faqs.question: How do I install Countly Community Edition?
faqs.answer: It can be installed with an automated bash script on Ubuntu/CentOS/RHEL, via Docker, or manually from stable release packages.

faqs.question: Which platforms does Countly support?
faqs.answer: SDKs are available for web, iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, Node.js, Unity, Java, and JavaScript, among others.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted mobile app analytics
use_cases.description: Small teams track app sessions, events, and crashes without sending data to a third-party SaaS.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious product analytics
use_cases.description: Organizations that need to keep analytics data on their own infrastructure for privacy or compliance reasons.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Countly Community Edition: free, self-hosted, open-source analytics with session, event, crash, and push notification tracking.

enrichment.short_description: MIT-licensed, GA4-compatible analytics platform that loads existing GA4/Matomo data into your own data warehouse.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: d8a.tech is an analytics platform built to work alongside existing GA4 or Matomo tracking implementations rather than replace them. It ingests the data those tools already collect and loads it into a reporting-ready schema inside a data warehouse the customer controls — supporting destinations such as BigQuery, ClickHouse, CSV, and Parquet — so organizations keep ownership of the raw data instead of only accessing it through a vendor dashboard.

The project is MIT licensed with code published on GitHub, and it can be deployed as a free EU-hosted cloud tier, self-hosted on a customer's own Kubernetes cluster, or air-gapped for isolated environments. It also includes a dashboard layer and an AI-assisted exploration feature for querying the normalized data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: d8a.tech targets organizations in regulated or data-sensitive sectors — healthcare, government, and financial services are called out specifically — that need to retain full control and sovereignty over analytics data collected via GA4 or Matomo without switching tracking tools.

features.title: Works with existing GA4/Matomo tracking
features.description: Ingests data from tracking implementations already in place without requiring new tagging.

features.title: Reporting-ready warehouse schema
features.description: Normalizes raw analytics events into flat tables designed for direct SQL analysis.

features.title: Multi-destination storage
features.description: Supports loading data into BigQuery, ClickHouse, CSV, or Parquet.

features.title: Historical GA4 import
features.description: Can import historical data from GA4 BigQuery exports.

features.title: AI-assisted exploration
features.description: Includes an AI agent for exploratory querying and insight discovery over the normalized data.

features.title: Flexible deployment
features.description: Available as EU-hosted cloud, self-hosted on Kubernetes, or air-gapped for isolated networks.

pros: MIT licensed and open source with self-hosting available
Keeps analytics data in a customer-owned warehouse instead of a vendor silo
Works with existing GA4/Matomo setups without re-tagging
Multiple deployment modes including air-gapped for compliance-heavy environments
cons: Requires an existing GA4 or Matomo implementation to source data from
Detailed paid-tier pricing is not published on the marketing site
Self-hosted/air-gapped deployment requires Kubernetes and data-engineering expertise

faqs.question: Is d8a.tech free?
faqs.answer: It offers a free EU-hosted cloud tier; self-hosted deployment is also available since the project is MIT licensed and open source.

faqs.question: Does d8a.tech replace Google Analytics tracking?
faqs.answer: No. It works alongside an existing GA4 or Matomo implementation and loads that data into a customer-owned warehouse rather than replacing the tracking layer.

faqs.question: Where can data be stored?
faqs.answer: Supported destinations include BigQuery, ClickHouse, CSV, and Parquet.

faqs.question: Can it be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, including on a customer's own Kubernetes cluster or in an air-gapped environment.

use_cases.title: Data sovereignty for regulated industries
use_cases.description: Healthcare, government, and financial organizations keep GA4-sourced analytics data inside infrastructure they control.

use_cases.title: Warehouse-native analytics
use_cases.description: Analysts query a normalized, reporting-ready schema directly with SQL instead of relying on a vendor's dashboard UI.

features: Deploy on your own Kubernetes cluster

enrichment.seo_meta_description: d8a.tech: MIT-licensed analytics platform that loads existing GA4/Matomo data into your own warehouse, self-hosted or cloud.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source web app that visualizes a GitHub repository's actual daily star, commit, and activity history over time.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Daily Stars Explorer is an open-source, MIT-licensed web application for exploring the history of any public GitHub repository. Unlike star-history tools that interpolate a straight line between two points in time, it plots actual daily star counts alongside commits, pull requests, issues, forks, and contributor activity, giving a more accurate picture of when a project gained or lost traction.

It is built with a Go backend and a JavaScript/TypeScript frontend, is hosted freely at emanuelef.github.io/daily-stars-explorer with no signup required, and is also distributed as a Docker image for self-hosting. The project is maintained on GitHub, where it has accumulated several hundred stars and accepts community contributions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It's aimed at open-source maintainers, developers evaluating libraries, and anyone researching how a GitHub project's popularity and activity evolved day by day rather than relying on a coarse, interpolated star-history chart.

features.title: Daily star history
features.description: Shows actual day-by-day star counts for a repository rather than an interpolated trend line.

features.title: Hourly tracking with timezone support
features.description: Can drill into hourly star activity with timezone-aware timestamps.

features.title: Repository comparison
features.description: Compares star and activity history side-by-side across multiple repositories.

features.title: Activity timelines
features.description: Charts commits, pull requests, issues, forks, and contributor activity over time.

features.title: Community mention feed
features.description: Surfaces mentions of a repository from Hacker News, Reddit, YouTube, and GitHub.

features.title: Data export
features.description: Exports underlying data as CSV or JSON.

features.title: Self-hosting via Docker
features.description: Ships an official Docker image for teams that want to run it themselves.

pros: Free, open source, and MIT licensed
Shows true daily data instead of interpolated star-history lines
No signup required to use the hosted version
Includes broader activity context (commits, PRs, issues, forks) beyond just stars
cons: Focused specifically on GitHub repository metrics, not general analytics
Self-hosting requires running the provided Docker image and managing GitHub API access
Relies on public GitHub data, so private repository insight is limited

faqs.question: Is Daily Stars Explorer free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free, open source, and MIT licensed, with a free hosted version and a self-hosted Docker option.

faqs.question: Do I need to sign up to use it?
faqs.answer: No signup is required to use the hosted version at emanuelef.github.io/daily-stars-explorer.

faqs.question: Can I compare multiple repositories?
faqs.answer: Yes, the tool supports side-by-side comparison of star and activity history across repositories.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Daily Stars Explorer?
faqs.answer: Yes, an official Docker image is provided for self-hosted deployment.

use_cases.title: Open-source growth tracking
use_cases.description: Maintainers track how a repository's star count and contributor activity actually grew day-by-day.

use_cases.title: Library due diligence
use_cases.description: Developers compare candidate libraries' historical activity before adopting one.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Daily Stars Explorer: free open-source tool showing a GitHub repo's real daily star, commit, and activity history.

enrichment.short_description: Privacy-first, MIT-licensed web analytics available as a managed cloud service or a single-binary self-hosted server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HitKeep is a web analytics platform built around data ownership and privacy. It tracks website traffic, conversions, and search performance without relying on cookies or third-party ad-network data sharing, and it distinguishes crawler traffic (including AI crawlers) from human visits. The product is offered both as a managed cloud service, with EU (Frankfurt) and US (Virginia) hosting regions, and as MIT-licensed, self-hostable software.

The self-hosted build runs as a single Go binary with an embedded DuckDB database and NSQ messaging, requiring no external database or container orchestration, and runs on Linux amd64/arm64. Cloud pricing is tiered by site count, team size, and data retention, while the self-hosted version is free to run indefinitely.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: HitKeep targets privacy-conscious teams, organizations that answer to a data protection officer, and agencies managing analytics for multiple client sites who want either EU/US data residency in the cloud or full control via self-hosting.

features.title: Cookieless tracking
features.description: Tracks visitors and conversions without cookies, configured by default to send no telemetry.

features.title: Goal and funnel tracking
features.description: Supports goal tracking, multi-step funnels, and ecommerce revenue measurement.

features.title: AI crawler differentiation
features.description: Distinguishes AI crawler traffic from human referral traffic for AI-visibility tracking.

features.title: Scheduled reports and dashboards
features.description: Sends scheduled email reports and supports shareable dashboards.

features.title: Webhooks and REST API
features.description: Provides signed outbound webhooks and a REST API for integrating with other systems.

features.title: Full data export
features.description: Exports data in JSON, CSV, Parquet, NDJSON, and XLSX formats.

features.title: Passkey authentication
features.description: Supports WebAuthn/passkey login and role-based access control.

features.title: Single-binary self-hosting
features.description: Self-hosted deployment runs as one Go binary with embedded DuckDB, no external database required.

pros: MIT licensed with a genuinely free, perpetual self-hosted option
No external database or orchestration needed for self-hosting
Cloud plans offer EU and US data residency
Full data export in multiple formats avoids lock-in
cons: Free cloud tier is limited to 3 sites and 60-day retention
Self-hosting requires comfort running and maintaining a Go binary on a server
Higher site counts and longer retention require paid Pro or Business plans

faqs.question: Is HitKeep free?
faqs.answer: The self-hosted version is free and MIT licensed. The cloud service has a free tier (3 sites, 60-day retention) plus paid Pro and Business plans.

faqs.question: Can HitKeep be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, it runs as a single Go binary with an embedded DuckDB database and no external database or container orchestration required.

faqs.question: Does HitKeep use cookies?
faqs.answer: No, it is configured by default with no cookies and no telemetry.

faqs.question: What does the Pro plan cost?
faqs.answer: The Pro plan is €150/year (or €15/month), covering 10 sites, 5 team members, and 1-year data retention.

use_cases.title: Cookieless site analytics
use_cases.description: Site owners track traffic and conversions without cookie banners or consent management overhead.

use_cases.title: Multi-client agency reporting
use_cases.description: Agencies manage analytics across many client sites with scheduled reports and shareable dashboards.

features: Single Go binary with embedded DuckDB

enrichment.seo_meta_description: HitKeep: privacy-first, MIT-licensed web analytics with cookieless tracking, free self-hosting, and a managed cloud option.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, cookieless European web and product analytics platform with AI-assisted reporting and self-hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Litlyx is a European, privacy-first analytics platform positioned as an alternative to Google Analytics. It tracks website traffic, product usage, SEO keywords, and marketing attribution without cookies or personal-data collection, aiming to be GDPR-compliant without requiring a cookie consent banner.

The project is open source on GitHub, with a self-hosting option in addition to its hosted cloud plans. Cloud pricing is based on monthly pageview volume, and the platform includes an AI-powered assistant for interpreting analytics data alongside real-time dashboards, custom events, funnels, and one-click branded reports.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Litlyx targets small agencies, freelancers, and solopreneurs managing multiple sites, as well as businesses that want GDPR-compliant analytics with a simpler setup than Google Analytics.

features.title: Cookieless tracking
features.description: Tracks visitors anonymously without cookies, avoiding the need for a consent banner.

features.title: Custom events and funnels
features.description: Supports defining custom events and analyzing conversion funnels.

features.title: AI data analyst
features.description: Includes an AI feature for interpreting analytics data and answering questions about it.

features.title: Real-time dashboards
features.description: Displays live traffic and event data in real-time dashboards.

features.title: Branded reports
features.description: Generates one-click, shareable branded reports.

features.title: SEO keyword tracking
features.description: Monitors SEO keyword performance alongside traffic analytics.

features.title: Multi-domain management
features.description: Manages analytics across multiple domains from one account.

features.title: Self-hosting
features.description: Open-source codebase can be self-hosted instead of using the hosted cloud plans.

pros: Open source with a self-hosting option
Cookieless tracking avoids consent-banner requirements
Integrates with Shopify, WordPress, Google Tag Manager, and Framer
Includes AI-assisted analysis on top of standard dashboards
cons: Paid cloud plans are priced by pageview volume, which can grow with traffic
AI features and higher data retention require paid plans
No published free-forever cloud tier beyond the entry Mini plan

faqs.question: Is Litlyx free?
faqs.answer: Litlyx is open source and can be self-hosted for free; the hosted cloud plans start at €8.99/month.

faqs.question: Does Litlyx require a cookie consent banner?
faqs.answer: No, it uses cookieless, anonymous tracking designed for GDPR compliance without a consent banner.

faqs.question: Can Litlyx be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, the platform is open source on GitHub with a self-hosting option.

faqs.question: What integrations does Litlyx support?
faqs.answer: It integrates with Shopify, WordPress, Google Tag Manager, and Framer.

use_cases.title: Cookieless website analytics
use_cases.description: Site owners get GDPR-friendly traffic analytics without a consent banner.

use_cases.title: Multi-site agency reporting
use_cases.description: Agencies manage analytics and branded reports across multiple client domains from one dashboard.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Litlyx: open-source, cookieless European analytics platform with AI reporting, self-hosting, and pageview-based pricing.

enrichment.short_description: Apache 2.0-licensed, single-binary self-hosted web analytics with no cookies, no database, and built-in bot filtering.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Liwan is a privacy-focused, self-hosted web analytics tool built in Rust with tokio for asynchronous I/O. It runs as a single, self-contained binary with no separate database dependency, tracking page views, visitor groups, and referrers without cookies, cross-site tracking, or persistent identifiers. All collected data stays on the operator's own server.

The project is fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license and is designed to run efficiently on modest hardware, including budget VPS instances, older computers, or a Raspberry Pi. It provides a real-time dashboard that updates automatically and includes built-in filtering of bot and crawler traffic.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Liwan suits developers and site operators who want lightweight, self-hosted analytics with strong data sovereignty and minimal server resource requirements, and who are comfortable deploying and maintaining a self-hosted binary.

features.title: Single-binary deployment
features.description: Ships as one self-contained binary with no external database dependency.

features.title: Cookieless tracking
features.description: Tracks visitors without cookies, cross-site tracking, or persistent identifiers.

features.title: Real-time dashboard
features.description: Updates traffic dashboards automatically in real time.

features.title: Bot and crawler filtering
features.description: Filters known bots and crawlers from visitor reports automatically.

features.title: Visitor group and referrer tracking
features.description: Tracks page views, visitor groups, and referrer sources.

features.title: Low resource footprint
features.description: Designed to run on budget VPS instances, older hardware, or a Raspberry Pi.

pros: Fully open source under the permissive Apache 2.0 license
No database to set up or maintain — single binary handles everything
Cookieless, privacy-respecting tracking with all data kept on your own server
Very low resource requirements, suitable for cheap or older hardware
cons: Self-hosting only — no managed cloud option
No published details on advanced features like funnels or custom event tracking
Requires comfort with server administration to deploy and maintain

faqs.question: Is Liwan free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Liwan is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license.

faqs.question: Does Liwan require a database?
faqs.answer: No, it runs as a single self-contained binary with no external database dependency.

faqs.question: Does Liwan use cookies?
faqs.answer: No, it tracks visitors without cookies, cross-site tracking, or persistent identifiers.

faqs.question: Can Liwan run on a Raspberry Pi?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is designed to be lightweight enough for budget VPS instances, older computers, or a Raspberry Pi.

use_cases.title: Lightweight self-hosted analytics

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Liwan: Apache 2.0-licensed, single-binary self-hosted web analytics with no cookies, no database, and bot filtering.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hostable privacy analytics with a sub-1KB tracker, no cookies, and no IP address collection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Medama Analytics is a self-hostable, privacy-focused website analytics platform. Its tracking script is under 1KB and does not use cookies or collect IP addresses, and the server is designed for a straightforward single-binary deployment that runs with minimal resources (as little as 256MB of memory) and no external dependencies.

The project targets GDPR and PECR compliance and exposes an OpenAPI-based server for integrating with its dashboard. It is described as open source and self-hostable, with no hosted/SaaS offering mentioned on the project's documentation site.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It is aimed at website owners and operators who want a minimal-footprint, self-hosted analytics tool that avoids cookies and IP collection for GDPR/PECR compliance, and who prefer full control over their analytics infrastructure.

features.title: No cookies, no IP collection

features.title: Real-time monitoring
features.description: Provides real-time analytics on website performance and user interactions.

features.title: GDPR/PECR-oriented design
features.description: Built with GDPR and PECR privacy compliance as a design goal.

features.title: OpenAPI server
features.description: Exposes an OpenAPI-based backend for dashboard and integration use.

features.title: Single-binary, low-resource deployment
features.description: Runs as a single binary requiring as little as 256MB of memory with no external dependencies.

Very lightweight tracker and low server resource requirements
No cookies or IP address collection by design
Simple single-binary self-hosted deployment
cons: Self-hosting only — no hosted/managed option documented
No published pricing since there is no commercial tier
Requires server administration knowledge to deploy and maintain

faqs.question: Is Medama Analytics free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is open source and self-hostable at no cost.

faqs.question: Does Medama Analytics collect IP addresses?
faqs.answer: No, its documentation states it does not collect IP addresses or use cookies.

faqs.question: How much server resource does Medama Analytics need?
faqs.answer: It's designed to run with minimal resources, as little as 256MB of memory, with no external dependencies.

faqs.question: Is there a hosted version of Medama Analytics?
faqs.answer: The documentation describes it as self-hostable software; no separate hosted SaaS option is documented.

use_cases.description: Website owners collect basic usage analytics while meeting GDPR/PECR requirements without cookies or IP logging.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Medama Analytics: open-source, self-hosted privacy analytics with a sub-1KB tracker, no cookies, no IP collection.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted web analytics that requires opt-in consent and gives users full access to their own data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Offen is an open-source, self-hosted web analytics tool built around a "fair analytics" model: tracking is opt-in only, all collected user data is end-to-end encrypted, and every visitor can view, export, or delete their own data at any time through a transparency dashboard. Development began in 2019 with support from the NLnet Foundation's Next Generation Internet initiative.

The project provides real-time metrics — page views, key metrics, top pages, and weekly retention — filterable by URL, location, referrer, UTM parameters, and landing/exit pages, and supports multiple websites within a single installation. Data is retained for a maximum of six months. Offen's stated commitment is that the core product will always remain free to use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Offen is built for website operators who want GDPR-compliant analytics based on genuine user consent and transparency rather than cookie-banner workarounds, and who are willing to self-host the platform themselves.

features.title: Opt-in only tracking
features.description: Visitors must actively consent before any data is collected; nothing is tracked by default.

features.title: End-to-end encryption
features.description: All collected user data is end-to-end encrypted.

features.title: User data transparency
features.description: Users can view, export, or delete their own collected data at any time.

features.title: Real-time reporting
features.description: Shows page views, key metrics, top pages, and weekly retention in real time.

features.title: Filterable analytics
features.description: Filters data by URL, location, referrer, UTM parameters, and landing/exit pages.

features.title: Multi-site support
features.description: Manages multiple websites from a single Offen installation.

features.title: Team sharing
features.description: Supports sharing analytics access with a team.

features.title: Customizable consent banner
features.description: Ships a customizable banner for collecting visitor opt-in consent.

pros: Free, open source, and self-hosted with no vendor lock-in
Genuine opt-in consent model rather than a cookie-banner workaround
End-to-end encrypted, user-accessible data builds real transparency
Multi-language interface support
cons: Opt-in-only tracking means lower data coverage than consent-agnostic analytics tools
Six-month data retention limit may not suit long-term trend analysis
Self-hosting required — no hosted SaaS version
Project activity/maintenance cadence should be checked before production use

faqs.question: Is Offen free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Offen is free, open source, and the project states it will always remain free to use.

faqs.question: Does Offen require cookie consent?
faqs.answer: Offen uses an opt-in-only model — visitors must actively consent before any tracking occurs, via a customizable consent banner.

faqs.question: How long does Offen retain data?
faqs.answer: Data retention is limited to a maximum of six months.

faqs.question: Can users see their own tracked data?
faqs.answer: Yes, users can view, export, or delete their own data at any time through a transparency dashboard.

use_cases.title: Consent-first website analytics
use_cases.description: Site operators collect usage analytics based on genuine visitor opt-in rather than a cookie banner that tracks by default.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Offen: free, open-source, self-hosted analytics with opt-in consent, end-to-end encryption, and user data transparency.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, GDPR-compliant web analytics built on Grafana and ClickHouse, available self-hosted or as a single-plan cloud.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Prisme Analytics is a privacy-focused web analytics platform positioned as an alternative to Google Analytics. It's built on top of Grafana for dashboards and ClickHouse for data storage, processes traffic in real time (versus the 24-48 hour delay typical of Google Analytics), and is designed to operate without a cookie consent banner.

The project is open source and available on GitHub, with the stated goal that all features available in Prisme Cloud are also available in the open-source, self-hosted distribution. The hosted Cloud offering uses a single all-inclusive pricing plan rather than tiered plans, billed monthly with a 30-day free trial and no credit card required to start.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Prisme Analytics suits website owners, content creators, agencies, and e-commerce businesses that want real-time, cookie-banner-free analytics with GDPR/CCPA/PECR compliance, plus the option to self-host to avoid vendor lock-in.

features.title: Real-time analytics
features.description: Processes traffic data in real time rather than with the multi-day delay typical of Google Analytics.

features.title: Bot and spam filtering
features.description: Filters bot and spam traffic to show human-only visit counts.

features.title: Custom dashboards
features.description: Built on Grafana, supporting funnels, histograms, and geomaps as custom visualizations.

features.title: Custom events
features.description: Tracks custom events beyond default pageview metrics.

features.title: SQL query API
features.description: Exposes a SQL query API for direct exploration of underlying ClickHouse data.

features.title: JavaScript-less tracking option
features.description: Supports tracking via a pixel GIF tag as an alternative to JavaScript.

features.title: UTM campaign tracking
features.description: Tracks campaign performance via UTM parameters.

features.title: No cookie banner required
features.description: Designed to meet GDPR/CCPA/PECR/Schrems II compliance without a cookie consent banner.

pros: Open source with self-hosting available to avoid vendor lock-in
Same feature set in open-source and cloud versions
Real-time reporting instead of a multi-day data delay
Single, all-inclusive pricing plan with unlimited websites and pageviews on Cloud
cons: Cloud has a single plan rather than lower-cost entry tiers
Self-hosting requires running and maintaining Grafana and ClickHouse infrastructure
SQL query API assumes some familiarity with ClickHouse/SQL for advanced use

faqs.question: Is Prisme Analytics open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source on GitHub and can be self-hosted with the same feature set as the paid Cloud offering.

faqs.question: How much does Prisme Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Prisme Cloud uses a single all-inclusive plan priced at €8.99/month, with a 30-day free trial and no credit card required.

faqs.question: Does Prisme Analytics require a cookie banner?
faqs.answer: No, it's designed for GDPR/CCPA/PECR/Schrems II compliance without requiring a cookie consent banner.

faqs.question: What is Prisme Analytics built on?
faqs.answer: It's built on Grafana for dashboards and ClickHouse for data storage.

use_cases.title: Cookie-banner-free site analytics
use_cases.description: Website owners get GDPR-compliant traffic analytics without showing a cookie consent banner.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted analytics with SQL access
use_cases.description: Technical teams self-host Prisme and query the underlying ClickHouse data directly via SQL.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Prisme Analytics: open-source, GDPR-compliant analytics on Grafana and ClickHouse, self-hosted or single-plan cloud.
